title: Squeezed state degradations due to mode mismatch and thermal aberrations in gravitational wave detectors
abstract: To date, frequency-dependent squeezed light has been used to reduce quantum noise in interferometric gravitational wave detectors by 6.1 dB (a factor of two). Future upgrades and detectors aim to both reduce quantum noise by 10 dB (a factor of three) and to increase the circulating power in the interferometer arm cavities. Achieving these goals will be extremely challenging due, in part, to the degradations to the squeezed state caused by mode mismatch between the internal interferometer optical cavities and between the auxiliary external cavities. It is therefore imperative to gain a detailed understanding of all sources of mismatch and to obtain experience in mitigating their effects in the current detectors in order to improve astrophysical sensitivity now and in the future. Two types of internal mismatch are identified which are due to the thermal aberrations generated when the test mass optics absorb a small fraction of the circulating arm power. It is found that the dynamics responsible for the degradations caused by the mismatch between the quadratic part of the wavefront of two modes has a characteristic low-pass frequency dependence while the dynamics of the mismatch due to all higher order thermal aberrations has a high-pass behavior. As a consequence, the two types of mismatch are predominantly responsible for different squeezing degradations -- some of which are significant for the current detectors and some of which will only be important for future detectors with longer arms. The behavior of these two types of internal mismatch are described and the implications for detector design, operation, and characterization are discussed.
summary: This paper investigates how thermal aberrations in gravitational wave detectors degrade the effectiveness of squeezed light used to reduce quantum noise. It distinguishes between quadratic wavefront mismatch and higher-order aberrations, showing they have different frequency-dependent impacts (low-pass vs. high-pass). The research provides a framework for characterizing current detectors and designing future, more powerful detectors like Cosmic Explorer. Ultimately, it helps identify the specific thermal effects that must be mitigated to reach the next generation of astrophysical sensitivity.
main_problem: Identifying and quantifying how squeezed state degradation in gravitational wave detectors is caused by internal mode mismatch, specifically due to thermal aberrations like quadratic wavefront mismatch and higher-order aberrations.
main_result: The study identifies that quadratic mismatch exhibits low-pass frequency dependence while higher-order thermal aberrations exhibit high-pass behavior, and demonstrates that the total squeezing degradation is highly sensitive to the full optical path difference profile.
method: The authors use a combination of analytical models (using ABCD matrices and operator graphs) and numerical simulations (using the Finesse package) to model the coupling between fundamental and higher-order modes.
model_or_system: A coupled cavity system representing a dual-recycled Fabry-Perot Michelson interferometer, including arm cavities, a signal extraction cavity (SEC), and external cavities for frequency-dependent squeezing.
key_observables: Squeezing degradation (in dB), frequency-dependent quantum noise reduction, and mode-mismatch-induced coupling.
important_parameters: Arm and SEC finesse, Gouy phase, power scattered into higher-order modes (Upsilon), and thermal aberration types (quadratic vs. higher-order).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a geometrically stable cavity, neglects external mode mismatch, and uses a simplified thin-lens approximation for thermal lensing.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the coupled cavity system and an operator graph; Figure 2 is a signal flow diagram; Figure 3 illustrates the optical path difference due to absorption; Figure 10 presents a detailed quantum noise budget.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the problem of squeezing degradation, establishes a mathematical framework for coupled cavity dynamics, decomposes thermal aberrations into two distinct types, analyzes their frequency-dependent dynamics, and concludes with a comprehensive noise budget and simulation for future detectors.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems researchers interested in how spatial mode mismatch and environmental-induced aberrations (thermal lensing) degrade the purity and utility of squeezed vacuum states in complex interferometric setups.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Coupled cavity equivalent to the differential arm motion of a gravitational wave detector. The ellipses and circles represent (potentially squeezed) quantum vacuum of the electromagnetic field at several locations. The 0 modes represent the fundamental vacuum and the 1 and subsequent modes represent the HOM vacuum. Several nodes of the system are marked with the µi labels. The squeezed states are injected at the node µas,i after being reflected off of the filter cavity (not shown but included in the analysis). The signal is detected at the node µas,r after passing through the output mode cleaner which ensures that only the fundamental mode is measured. The power absorbed in the...

Figure 2. Coupled cavity signal flow diagram for the model of the fundamental and a single HOM described in Section II B. This system can be thought of as two separate coupled cavities which are coupled by the mismatch between the SEC and arm cavity at the ITM. As explained by Eq. (24), the HOM SEC is AC coupled with the fundamental SEC through higher order aberrations while being DC coupled through quadratic mismatch. The vacuum and important nodes from Fig. 1 are reproduced here with the superscripts 0 and 1 to indicate the fundamental and HOM, respectively. Rather than drawing the arm cavities in full, the fundamental arm cavity ra0(Ω) is given by Eq. (4) with δωa = 0 and the HOM arm...

Figure 3. Optical path length differences resulting from the propagation through the substrate of a LIGO-like test mass due to thermal aberrations generated by the uniform absorption of a 4σ = 10.6 cm diameter beam. Computed using the ax- isymmetric Hello-Vinet [14] model. The equivalent quadratic thin lens is shown along with the residual OPD relative to this lens. We refer to the aberrations generated by these dis- tortions as quadratic mismatch and higher order aberrations (HOA), respectively. We parameterize the HOA mismatch by the equivalent OPD relative to the perfect compensation of an optic which may have of order one watt absorbed in the coatings. This parameterization thus allows...

Figure 4. Quantum noise budget for Cosmic Explorer with ∆w/w = 5 % and Prel = 100 mW using the parameters of Table II. Note that this is a displacement, rather than strain, budget. The peaks in the noise above 800 Hz are due to higher order mode resonances in the arm cavities which both rotate the squeezed state and induce up to 520 mrad of intrinsic phase noise; see Section IV B.

Figure 4 shows a budget of the quantum noises in Cos- mic Explorer using the baseline parameters of Table II. We will describe the squeezing degradations by the Mc- Culler squeezing metrics [12]. The details of using these four metrics to make noise budgets such as Fig. 4 are given in Appendix A. We first give a brief overview of these metrics and how mode mismatch contributes to them before delving into the details of how they quantify the effects of several sources of squeezing degradations in the following sections. Note that while we focus on mode mis- match, the HOMs responsible for misalignment produce squeezing degradations just as the HOMs responsible for mode mismatch do, and so...

Figure 5. Direct mode mismatch loss for ∆w/w = 5 % and Prel = 100 mW along with both types of aberrations separately for LIGO A♯using the parameters of Table II. The dashed lines do not include radiation pressure and correspond to the discussion in Section IV A 1 while the solid lines include radiation pressure as discussed in Section IV A 2. The low-pass nature of quadratic mismatch and high-pass nature of higher order aberrations is evident, along with the fact that higher order modes can interfere destructively to produce less loss. 500 ppm of SEC loss is also shown for comparison.

Figure 6. Mode mismatch loss for Cosmic Explorer varying Prel while keeping ∆w/w = 5 % as a function of SEC Gouy phase. The vertical dashed lines mark the 8th order resonance at 22.5◦and the 6th order resonance at 30◦in the absence of thermal aberrations and apertures. The Gouy phase is the one-way Gouy phase as computed from the round-trip ABCD matrix of the SEC; see Appendix B. Recall Fig. 3 and the fact that Prel can be negative in our parameterization.

Figure 7. Squeezed state rotation around the first HOM arm cavity resonance in A♯with a SEM transmissivity of Ts = 20 % for slightly different thermal states. This exact behavior is well accounted for by Eq. (53). The RSE pole in this case is 750 Hz and the SR pole is 2 Hz. Fits to these curves give widths of between 6 and 10 Hz showing that the dynamics are characteristic of those of a signal recycled interferometer for the fundamental mode. Measuring this rotation with an audio diagnostic field [51] may prove useful in characterizing the detectors and optimizing their configurations as discussed in Section IV B 3.

Figure 8. Higher order mode resonances in CE for two different arm cavity geometries with 70 cm ∅arm cavity apertures. Only second order mode resonances are significant after the addition of apertures. The free spectral range is 3750 Hz and the transverse mode spacing is 1460 Hz for the w = 12 cm case and 1110 Hz for the w = 13 cm case. In both cases, the first peak is due to the second order resonance in the first FSR, the second peak is due to the same resonance in the zeroth FSR, and the third peak is due to the resonance in the second FSR.

Figure 9. Broadband rotation of the squeezed state. The peaks are due to the HOM arm cavity resonances discussed in Sec- tion IV B superimposed on the broadband rotation described by Eq. (59). The dashed lines do not include radiation pressure and thus only illustrate the purely optical rotation described by Eq. (59). The solid lines include radiation pressure and thus also include the rotation due to the shifted SQL frequency.
